name: Dominica

colors.symbolism: the lush vegetation and forests of Dominica
the sunshine, the country's main agricultural products (citrus and bananas), and the Carib (Kalinago) people, the original inhabitants
the rich black soil of the island and Dominicans of African descent
the rivers and waterfalls of the island and the purity of aspirations
the central red disc represents social justice
the body of the sisserou parrot

description: A green field crossed by three narrow stripes — yellow, black and white — running vertically and horizontally to form a centred Greek-cross pattern. At the intersection is a red disc bearing a sisserou parrot in left-facing profile, encircled by ten five-pointed green stars.
history: Adopted on 3 November 1978 when Dominica gained independence from the United Kingdom. Designed by Alwin Bully, the flag has been amended three times — in 1981, 1988 and 1990 — chiefly to refine the colours and orientation of the parrot. The 1990 revision standardised the current arrangement: the parrot now faces the hoist (its left), the stars are bordered in green, and the red disc bears the parrot directly without a yellow inner border. Dominica is one of very few nations whose flag depicts a specifically national bird species.

features.symbolism: a green field charged with a centred cross of three thin stripes (yellow, black, white) and a red disc at the centre bearing a sisserou parrot encircled by ten green stars

features.role: vertical and horizontal cross of three colours
features.symbolism: the cross — formed of three thin stripes, yellow, black and white in succession — represents the Trinity and the Christian faith of the people; the three arms of the cross meet to form a Greek cross extending to the edges of the field

the national bird, found only on Dominica; symbolising flight toward greater heights and the fulfilment of aspiration. It is one of only two national flags in the world to feature the colour purple prominently

features.role: ring of ten stars around the parrot
features.symbolism: the ten parishes of Dominica, all with equal status; the green border around each star ties them to the national colour
